title: US-Iran Ceasefire Collapse Sends Oil Surging as Central Banks Diverge on Rates
subhead: The US-Iran ceasefire officially collapsed this week after Iran attacked commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz, sending Brent crude above $78 and reviving inflation fears — just as the Fed's new chair signals a hawkish turn and the BOJ hits a 31-year high on rates. Meanwhile, Canada and the UK escalated sanctions on Russia's shadow fleet at the G7, China extended its gold-buying streak to 20 months, and Asia's energy importers tightened currency defenses.

sections.heading: US-Iran Ceasefire Collapses Over Strait of Hormuz Attacks
sections.body:
The US-Iran ceasefire formally collapsed this week after Iran attacked commercial vessels in the Strait of Hormuz, including strikes on three Qatari and Saudi tankers in Omani waters. President Trump declared the ceasefire 'OVER' on Friday, though both sides agreed to continue talks. The US responded with fresh airstrikes on Iranian targets, and Iran's new supreme leader Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei pledged vengeance for his father's killing.

Strait of Hormuz traffic has plummeted — only 22 ships transited on Thursday, compared to more than 130 per day before the war, according to maritime data firm Kpler. Iran insists its territorial waters are the only viable route and has attacked vessels using the Omani corridor protected by the US military. Oman has drafted a proposal for free navigation through the southern corridor in Omani waters, while the northern corridor through Iranian waters would require Iran's prior approval, according to CNN.

The US revoked the license authorizing Iranian crude sales on Tuesday after the ship attacks. Brent crude closed the week near $78/barrel, up 5.2% on July 8 alone — the 10th-largest one-day move during the conflict. WTI settled at $73.52/barrel. Prices remain above prewar levels despite having fallen from the $120 peak during the war's worst phase.
sections.whyItMatters: The Hormuz disruption is no longer a temporary shock — it's becoming a persistent feature. With daily traffic at under 20% of prewar levels and Iran attacking ships in Omani waters, the market is repricing energy risk. The US revocation of Iran's oil sales license removes one of the few economic incentives Tehran had to comply, making a durable resolution less likely in the near term.

sections.heading: Fed Chair Warsh Signals Hawkish Shift as Rate Hike Bets Surge
sections.body:
Federal Reserve Chair Kevin Warsh convened his first FOMC meeting with a clear hawkish tilt, describing internal divisions over inflation as a 'family fight' — but one where nine of 18 dot-plot participants expect at least one rate hike if inflation persists. CME FedWatch data shows September rate hike odds above 50%, with 50.8% pricing a 25bp increase. Just one month ago, 62.5% of traders anticipated a pause; that figure has collapsed to 34%.

Interest rate swap markets now price roughly 32 basis points of rate increases by year-end, implying one or two quarter-point hikes across the four remaining FOMC meetings. However, the latest US jobs data showed a sharp slowdown in June, complicating the hawkish case. Wells Fargo strategist Eric Nelson noted signs of 'fatigue in the recent [dollar] rally,' adding that July hike probability has fallen compared with earlier expectations.

Bullish dollar bets reached $40 billion as of June 30, the most optimistic reading since 2015, according to CFTC data — driven by both safe-haven demand from Iran tensions and the repricing of Fed expectations.
sections.whyItMatters: Warsh's Fed is steering toward tightening in an economy showing job-market softness and geopolitical uncertainty — a combination that narrows the policy window. If the Iran conflict keeps oil elevated, inflation pressures may force the Fed's hand even as growth slows, creating a stagflationary bind.

sections.heading: BOJ Raises Rates to 31-Year High as Yen Weakness Fuels Inflation
sections.body:
The Bank of Japan raised interest rates to their highest level in 31 years in a landmark policy normalization step, signaling readiness to tighten further. The move comes as Japan's wholesale inflation hit a three-year high in June, driven by fuel costs and a weak yen trading near 161-162 against the dollar. Reuters reports that the BOJ may also revise up its growth forecast for fiscal 2026, maintaining vigilance on inflation risk as AI-driven demand and a depreciating yen offset some of the relief from falling oil prices.

Crédit Agricole forecasts USD/JPY risks tilting toward 170, meaning further yen weakness is possible. Japanese Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama said the government wants to explore ways to encourage the Government Pension Investment Fund (GPIF) to boost domestic investment — a potential structural shift in capital allocation that could support the yen over time.
sections.whyItMatters: Japan is the world's largest creditor nation and a major holder of US Treasuries. A BOJ tightening cycle pushes JGB yields higher, which could trigger repatriation flows and tighten global liquidity. The yen at 162 also raises competitive devaluation concerns at a moment when G7 leaders are already debating trade imbalances.

sections.heading: G7 Escalates Sanctions on Russia While Debating China Trade
sections.body:
At the G7 summit in Evian-les-Bains, Canada announced new sanctions targeting 162 individuals, entities, and vessels linked to Russia's shadow fleet and energy sector, following a meeting between PM Mark Carney and President Zelensky. The UK separately unveiled a sanctions package targeting Russia's shadow oil fleet and seized a Russian-linked vessel in the English Channel — the first such seizure by British forces. Russian shadow fleet tankers are now taking longer routes to avoid the Channel, according to reports.

On the China front, Carney held a 'short but intense' discussion with Trump about Chinese EV tariffs, while Japanese PM Sanae Takaichi pushed G7 allies for a united front against Beijing's use of export controls and trade leverage. Takaichi also proposed a joint critical minerals stockpiling initiative to counter China's dominance in rare earths. The EU warned that diversifying away from China will require significant funding, with Beijing cautioning it would fight any EU moves to protect domestic industries.

Meanwhile, the US held off blacklisting China's DeepSeek and over 100 flagged Chinese firms to avoid escalating tensions with Beijing, signaling a calibrated approach to tech controls.
sections.whyItMatters: The G7 is operating on two tracks: tightening the economic vise on Russia while trying to coordinate a collective response to China's trade practices. Takaichi's minerals stockpile proposal and the EU's funding dilemma show the gap between strategic ambition and fiscal capacity. The US decision to delay blacklisting DeepSeek reveals the tension between national security hawks and those seeking to avoid further escalation with Beijing.

sections.heading: Central Banks Accelerate Gold Buying as Dedollarization Deepens
sections.body:
The People's Bank of China extended its gold-buying streak to 20 consecutive months in June, adding 480,000 ounces (15 metric tons) — its largest monthly purchase since resuming accumulation in November 2024. China's total gold reserves now stand at 75.44 million ounces. The purchases came as gold prices fell from record highs earlier this year, with the PBOC acting as a long-term allocator rather than a short-term trader, according to Fudan University economist Shao Yu.

China's forex reserves declined by $26 billion (0.75%) to $3.42 trillion in June, partly due to a stronger dollar. A World Gold Council survey found that a record share of central banks expect to keep increasing gold reserves over the next 12 months, citing a volatile international landscape. Nikkei Asia reports that 84% of survey respondents expect gold's share of global reserves to increase over the next five years while dollar holdings decline.

Central banks are also rethinking where they store their gold — increasingly shifting reserves to domestic vaults to ensure greater control, according to CNBC and the Wall Street Journal.
sections.whyItMatters: The PBOC's 20-month gold streak and the broader central bank buying trend signal a structural shift in reserve management. With 89% of central banks expecting to increase gold holdings, this is no longer a tactical play — it's a coordinated hedge against a dollar-centric system that they see as increasingly risky. The move toward domestic storage adds a physical sovereignty dimension that goes beyond portfolio allocation.

sections.heading: ECB Holds Firm on Inflation as Energy Shock Complicates Policy
sections.body:
ECB chief economist Philip Lane said the central bank will remain proactive in fighting high inflation despite falling energy prices following the US-Iran deal, signaling rates may stay elevated. The ECB previously raised rates and may pause at its upcoming meeting before delivering another hike in September, according to Livemint's macro analysis.

The Iran war's inflationary impact is still working through global supply chains. Mint's data analysis shows that global inflation has shown signs of firming up after a year of deflationary trends, with the conflict's impact on input costs persisting even as Brent crude has fallen from its $120 peak. The IMF underscored lingering risks for growth and inflation from the West Asia conflict, while Bloomberg Economics warned of continued downside pressure.

Aluminium supply disruptions from the Iran war are already sparking old Western smelters back into life, Reuters reported — a sign that supply chains are physically reshuffling in response to the conflict.
sections.whyItMatters: The ECB faces the same dilemma as the Fed: energy-driven inflation from a conflict that could escalate further, combined with weakening growth indicators. Lane's explicit commitment to stay proactive — even as oil falls — signals that the central bank has learned the 2021 lesson about not dismissing supply shocks as transitory. The aluminium smelter restarts show the real economy adapting faster than policy.

sections.heading: Asian Economies Tighten Currency Defenses Amid Energy Shock
sections.body:
South Korea and Indonesia are tightening oversight of speculative currency derivatives trades as the Iran war-driven oil disruption rattles forex markets. Both countries are heavily reliant on imported energy and are vulnerable to current account deterioration when oil spikes.

Indonesia officially launched its B50 biodiesel program, mandating 50% crude palm oil blend in diesel fuel — a move the government says could save Rp177 trillion ($10.9 billion) in foreign exchange by eliminating diesel imports. The program is both an energy security measure and a currency defense, reducing the country's exposure to dollar-denominated oil purchases.

Singapore's non-oil domestic exports surged 38.4% year-on-year in May, fueled by AI-related shipments, signaling sustained Asian tech supply chain momentum even amid global trade uncertainties.
sections.whyItMatters: Energy-importing Asian economies are the first to feel the currency pressure from the Hormuz disruption. Indonesia's B50 program is a tangible hedge — substituting palm oil for dollar-denominated diesel — while Korea's derivatives crackdown shows how quickly capital flow management can tighten. These are early-warning signals for how emerging markets respond when energy supply risk translates into balance-of-payments stress.

bottomLine: The US-Iran ceasefire collapsed this week, and the Strait of Hormuz is now operating at under 20% of prewar traffic — a level that makes the oil price premium structural rather than temporary. Brent at $78 is well below the war's $120 peak but firmly above prewar levels, and the market is repricing for a protracted disruption. This comes at exactly the wrong moment for central banks: the Fed under Warsh is shifting hawkish with September rate hike odds above 50%, the BOJ just hit a 31-year high on rates, and the ECB is signaling it won't ease despite falling energy prices. The G7 tightened Russia sanctions and debated China trade coordination, but the strategic gap between ambition and fiscal capacity remains wide. Meanwhile, the PBOC's 20-month gold streak and the global central bank rush into bullion confirm that reserve managers are hedging against the dollar system — not rhetorically, but with real money. The week's developments point to a world where energy supply risk, monetary tightening, and geopolitical fragmentation are compounding simultaneously.
sources: Al-Monitor, NYT, Reuters, Bloomberg, FXEmpire/CME FedWatch, China Daily, Seeking Alpha, Nikkei Asia, World Gold Council, CNBC, Livemint, Herald Business Korea, Kpler, CNBC, WSJ, Antara News, Tempo.co, Frontion Headlines
